result:
The first thing to notice is the sound of the refrigerator. It is not a single sound but a small choir: a low, steady hum that might be the compressor, a higher, almost metallic whine that could be the fan, and, every so often, a soft click like a distant typewriter carriage return. These sounds have been going on for so long—years, decades—that they have become the unofficial soundtrack of the kitchen, maybe of the whole house. If you woke someone in the middle of the night and played these sounds alone, they might instantly know where they were, the way sailors once recognized their ship by the creak of its timbers. The refrigerator is a private ocean, cooling and circulating, keeping its own time.

I have been listening to it because I have decided to sit at the kitchen table until I write two thousand five hundred words about whatever I want. That sentence feels illicit, like a forged hall pass: “whatever I want.” Most of the writing I do is tethered to obligation—emails, documentation, the polite choreography of making a living. The phrase “whatever I want” is a small, brightly colored door in a gray corridor. I am curious about what will step through it.

The table is the one my partner and I bought from an antique store that was going out of business. It is oak, scarred, heavier than any sane table should be. When we brought it home, we had to remove the front door from its hinges to wiggle the tabletop inside. The grain still carries the ripples of a century of spilled coffee, homework, midnight arguments, maybe even love letters. Wood is patient; it keeps the fingerprints of everyone who ever leaned on it, and then it waits. I rest my forearms on those fingerprints now, trespassing on strangers’ memories.

Outside, the neighborhood is practicing its late-morning quiet. A lawn mower starts, stalls, starts again, like someone clearing his throat repeatedly. A dog protests the indignity of being walked on a leash. Somewhere a screen door slaps its frame with the airy sound of summer permission. These are the sounds of a life I did not consciously choose but somehow slipped into, the way one might wake up inside a novel whose first hundred pages are missing. I like it here; I simply cannot remember choosing it.

The impulse to write without an assignment feels almost prehistoric, like discovering a rotary phone in the attic and wondering what dial tone still hums beneath the numbers. When I was eight, I wrote a newspaper called The Daily Owl on sheets of ruled paper torn from a spiral notebook. It had headlines like “Mom Misplaces Keys Again” and “Goldfish Not Dead Yet.” I sold single copies to my parents for a nickel. The pleasure then was absolute: I invented a world, set it down, and the very act felt like enough of a reason. No one asked about target audience, word count, or brand voice. I was the audience, the brand, the voice. I typed “The End” and meant it.

Years later, a writing professor told our workshop that we must “write with an ideal reader in mind.” I imagined a tall, solemn man in a tweed coat who would nod slowly at every sophisticated metaphor. The image froze me. I kept glancing over my shoulder to see if he approved, and my prose grew constipated with caution. Eventually he took up permanent residence in my head, tapping his watch, sighing whenever I got interested in something disreputable. I never managed to evict him until tonight, when I opened a blank document and typed “Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.” The phrase feels like a riot act. I picture the solemn man looking puzzled, then gradually removing his coat, loosening his tie, rolling up his sleeves. Maybe he too wants to see what will happen if no one is steering.

So I will write about the refrigerator, the table, the lawn mower, the imaginary newspaper, and anything else that arrives unbidden. I will write about the small jar of mustard that sits on the second shelf of the fridge door like a minor planet. It is Dijon, but not the posh kind with whole seeds; it is the supermarket variety that costs two dollars when it is on sale. We have had it for an embarrassingly long time. Mustard, unlike milk, does not march obediently toward expiration. It mutters in the corner, souring by microns, plotting. Sometimes I open the jar just to stare at its yellow surface, glossy as a pond at sunrise. No mold, no dark mysteries—only the faint ring of dried condiment around the rim, a miniature canyon geology. I wonder if, centuries from now, archaeologists will discover a potsherd of this very jar and build an exhibit around it: “Early twenty-first-century condiment rituals.” They will never guess that we simply forgot it was there.

The mustard jar reminds me of my father, who believed that any food capable of surviving a nuclear winter deserved respect. Our childhood fridge was a museum of almost-empty bottles: steak sauce with sepia crusts at the neck, jelly whose surface skin wrinkled like an old balloon, salad dressing separated into strata of oil and despair. Every now and then my mother would declare war and hurl the outrages into the trash. My father, offended, would rescue half of them, rinse the bottles, and pretend they were still viable. “Waste not, want not,” he’d say, as though the aphorism itself could re-emulsify thousand-island dressing. When he died, we found a jar of horseradish dated three presidents earlier. My mother held it up like evidence at a trial, then burst out laughing. Grief does odd things; sometimes it looks like hilarity squeezed between ribs.

I have seen grief arrive in other disguises. Once, at a reading in a small Midwestern library, a woman in the front row wept quietly through a story that had exactly zero tragic content. The story was about a man obsessed with cloud identification. Afterward I asked if she was okay. She said, “My husband used to lie in the backyard and name clouds. He died last spring. I didn’t know anyone else did that.” I felt simultaneously honored and fraudulent, as if I had pickpocketed her sorrow by accident. That is the secret economy of art: you think you are making something out of nothing, but the nothing is crowded with strangers’ memories, all waiting for a signal flare.

Back in the kitchen, the refrigerator hum shifts key, a half-tone lower. Maybe the thermostat has clicked off; maybe the appliance is surrendering to the afternoon heat. I remember reading that the universe’s background radiation is sometimes described as a “hum” leftover from the big bang. Every kitchen, then, is a smaller universe cooling into its own shape. I place my palm on the fridge side and feel the vague tremor, the way you might rest a hand on a horse’s flank to know it is alive. If I stood here long enough, would I begin to understand entropy by touch? Or would I simply grow cold and foolish-looking?

There is a science-fiction story in which time travel is possible only through old appliances. You climb inside a 1952 Kelvinator and emerge in the year the refrigerator was manufactured, blinking under neon signs advertising cigarettes at ten cents a pack. The twist is that you cannot bring anything back with you except the smell of whatever food was stored that week. Whole black-market economies arise around the odors of vanished summers: heirloom tomatoes before monoculture, peaches that still carried the phantom fur of bumblebees. Historians bottle the smells, archive them, argue over provenance. Meanwhile, smugglers try to monetize nostalgia by selling “1952 peach” to wealthy anosmics who want to know what loss feels like without the actual fragrance. I cannot decide if this is the saddest or most beautiful form of time travel I have ever heard of.

I wonder what smell my current refrigerator would carry. Probably the ghost of last week’s Thai takeout, notes of basil and cardboard fused in melancholy harmony. Perhaps the cucumber I bought optimistically and then watched slump into surrender. There is also the half onion wrapped in plastic, weeping quietly. If I opened the door now and leaned in, I might disappear into 2023, rematerialize on a day when I argued about recycling, when the cat vomited on the rug, when the news announced another war. Would I step out willingly? Or would I pull the door shut from the inside, choosing the dim glow of the bulb and the orderly rows of possible meals over whatever grief waited beyond?

I think of the people who have sat at this oak table before me. The antique-store owner said it came from a farm auction in rural Illinois, but he had no names, no anecdotes. I have supplied them anyway: a woman kneading bread in 1932, wrists deep in dough, while dust storms darken the windows; a boy spreading coloring books in 1967, his older brother away in Vietnam; a grandmother wrapping Christmas presents in 1984, humming to an old gospel station. I imagine their fingerprints under mine, overlapping like translucent maps. If the table could speak, would it brag about the weight it has held? Or would it whisper, “Please, move gently; I still remember every scratch”?

Objects outlive us; that much is obvious. What surprises me is how frequently they outlive our ability to describe them. My partner’s grandfather once carved a tiny wooden boat for her when she was six. The boat is no bigger than a bar of soap, painted a faded navy, with a white toothpick for a mast. It sits on our windowsill, and every time I glance at it, I try to summon the hands that carved it. I cannot. Those hands are gone, replaced by an artifact that gestures toward them like a shadow puppet. Someday the boat will sit on someone else’s sill, and my partner will be the unremembered ancestor. The thought should depress me, but instead it lands like a dare: while I am here, can I make something small and durable enough to carry a sliver of me forward? Maybe that is why any of us writes: not for immortality but for the chance to leave behind a toothpick mast, a mustard jar, an echo the future can misinterpret in whatever way it needs.

Outside, the lawn mower finally catches and roars into sustainable operation. Someone is cutting the grass that was, only yesterday, studded with violets. I love violets because they refuse to ask permission. They appear overnight, squatting between sidewalk cracks, flaunting purple like teenagers in hoodies. The neighbors fight them with herbicides, determined to maintain monocultural emerald. I root for the violets in secret. I want them to infiltrate, to dye the whole lawn until the homeowner’s association weeps into its bylaws. Rebellion can be microscopic; sometimes it is only a flower the size of a thumbnail insisting on existence.

The violets remind me of my first “publication.” In fifth grade I submitted a poem to the PTA newsletter. It was titled “Spring Things” and consisted of eight lines of excruciating rhyme: “Birds singy, bees stingy, everything is springy.” The newsletter printed it without comment, but the principal—an avuncular man with coffee breath—took me aside and said, “You have the instinct.” I didn’t know what the instinct was, but I liked the weight it placed in my chest, like a coin swallowed to smuggle across a border. Maybe that coin is still lodged somewhere, still glinting when the light hits just right. I wonder what Mr. Hadley the principal would make of this moment, thirty-five years later, me sitting at a stranger’s table writing about refrigerators and violets. Would he nod and say, “Yes, that instinct”? Or would he look embarrassed, the way adults do when a child’s drawing turns out to resemble something indecent they didn’t intend?

Instinct is a slippery creature. It once told me to quit a secure job and move across the country for love. It once told me to keep driving after twenty hours on the road, only to narrowly miss a deer that stepped onto the asphalt like an extra in its own myth. Instinct whispers, “Touch the stove; maybe it isn’t hot this time.” It also whispers, “Write the thing that makes you squint.” I have followed both commands with equal enthusiasm, which may explain why my knees are scarred and my notebooks are full of nonsense. Yet here I am, alive, typing, still listening. The refrigerator clicks again, perhaps seconding the motion.

Have you ever tried to count the sounds in a single room? Let’s inventory: the laptop fan cycling up, a cicada drilling the air outside, the soft tick of the wall clock that is always three minutes slow, the fridge, the lawn mower now receding down the block, the neighbor’s sprinkler going ts-ts-ts like a slow leak in morse code. Underneath all this, a faint high note that might be tinnitus or might be the universe screaming in a frequency only mammals lose the ability to hear. If I unplugged every device, if I imposed absolute silence, would the room swell with the roar of my own blood? I am tempted to try, but the document glows, waiting for word 1,647, and I remain tethered to the screen like a sailor to the mast.

I read somewhere that the average person speaks about sixteen thousand words a day. Most of those are spent on demands and pleasantries: “Pass the salt,” “Your email found me well.” By nightfall we have a few hundred words left for tenderness, a few dozen for revelation, maybe none to spare for ourselves. Writing reverses that economy. I can hoard words all day, refuse to speak at breakfast, grunt through lunch, and then spill an entire day’s ration into a page at 11:59 p.m. Friends call me quiet; they do not realize I am withholding. The page is my riot, my pent-up sixteen thousand allowed to mutate into form: complaint, hymn, manifesto, nonsense. “Whatever you want” is therefore a dangerous grant. It asks me to speak without spending the coin of ordinary conversation. It risks solipsism. It risks confession. It risks the possibility that once I begin I will discover I have nothing to say after all, only sounds: fridge hum, lawn mower, the small wet click of my own swallow.

I accept the risk. I accept the possibility that these words will amount to nothing more than a long diary entry I will never reread. Even that is a form of permanence: kilobytes stored on a server farm in an undisclosed state, spinning on a disk that will outlive the refrigerator, the oak table, even me. Sometime in 2089, a backup algorithm may clone this file into a colder bunker. A future technician might skim the first lines, shrug, and move on. That is okay. I am not writing for him. I am writing for the exact temperature of this Tuesday, for the smell of an onion aging in plastic, for the violets massing underground while blades whine above them. I am writing because once, when I was small, I drew a treasure map on a brown grocery bag, and when my mother asked where the treasure was, I pointed to the X and said, “Here, but it’s invisible.” She laughed and taped the map to the fridge. It hung there for years, curling at the edges, until we stopped noticing it was there. The treasure stayed invisible; the map stayed true.

My partner just walked through the room, glanced at the screen, and asked, “What are you writing?” I said, “Whatever I want.” She raised an eyebrow, amused or concerned—it is often both—and left me to it. That is love too: the willingness to let someone disappear into a room and make strange noises at a keyboard, trusting that eventually they will emerge, blinking, starved, maybe better, maybe not. I have learned not to apologize for the hours when language devours me. I return with souvenirs: a sentence that feels like skipping stones, a metaphor that clicks like the fridge at midnight. I hand them over like sea glass; she arranges them on the sill with the tiny boat. We build our shared museum incrementally: ticket stubs, violets pressed in library books, the echo of an old newspaperman saying, “You have the instinct.”

Word 2,107. The wall clock is now four minutes slow; I have watched it stall twice. I could stand up, find a battery, restore order. Instead I let it lag. It is comforting to live inside a room that refuses official time. Maybe that is what “whatever you want” ultimately offers: permission to be out of sync. The world demands synchronization—calendars, deadlines, algorithms that refresh every millisecond. Writing says, “Linger.” Writing says, “Fall behind.” Writing says, “Remember the taste of 1952 peaches you never actually ate.” The fridge hums on, indifferent, coolant circulating like dark matter. I rest my fingers on the keys, the way one might rest a forehead against a train window, watching landscapes rearrange themselves according to laws that do not apply once you step off the carriage.

I have 318 words left. I feel the way I do on long flights when the captain announces, “Thirty minutes to landing,” and suddenly every cloud below looks significant. I want to descend gracefully, wheels kissing tarmac, but I also want to stay suspended in the hum of possibility. The truth is, I could keep writing. I could describe the dust motes, the history of mustard, the etymology of the word “oak.” I could list every sound from now until dawn. But the assignment—if it is an assignment—asks for 2,500, and some stubborn ledger in my head insists on obedience. Perhaps that is the final revelation: even “whatever you want” arrives with edges. Freely given does not mean infinitely given. The refrigerator stops humming when you unplug it. The violets succumb to frost. The essay ends.

Yet before I land, let me offer one last invisible treasure: tomorrow, when I reread this, I will cringe at half the sentences. I will notice typos, repetitions, metaphors that wobble like drunken stilts. But somewhere in the middle—maybe around the paragraph about time-travel smells—future-me will pause and feel the exact weight of Tuesday’s light on the table. For one brief second, she will be back inside this room, palm on the fridge, listening. That is enough. That is the X on the map. The rest is onion skins, violet petals, the small boat forever sailing across a windowsill of someone else’s making.

Word 2,500.

Writing says, “Linger.” Writing says, “Fall behind.” Writing says, “Remember the taste of 1952 peaches you never actually ate.” The fridge hums on, indifferent, coolant circulating like dark matter. I rest my fingers on the keys, the way one might rest a forehead against a train window, watching landscapes rearrange themselves according to laws that do not apply once you step off the carriage.

I have 318 words left. I feel the way I do on long flights when the captain announces, “Thirty minutes to landing,” and suddenly every cloud below looks significant. I want to descend gracefully, wheels kissing tarmac, but I also want to stay suspended in the hum of possibility. The truth is, I could keep writing. I could describe the dust motes, the history of mustard, the etymology of the word “oak.” I could list every sound from now until dawn. But the assignment—if it is an assignment—asks for 2,500, and some stubborn ledger in my head insists on obedience. Perhaps that is the final revelation: even “whatever you want” arrives with edges. Freely given does not mean infinitely given. The refrigerator stops humming when you unplug it. The violets succumb to frost. The essay ends.

Yet before I land, let me offer one last invisible treasure: tomorrow, when I reread this, I will cringe at half the sentences. I will notice typos, repetitions, metaphors that wobble like drunken stilts. But somewhere in the middle—maybe around the paragraph about time-travel smells—future-me will pause and feel the exact weight of Tuesday’s light on the table. For one brief second, she will be back inside this room, palm on the fridge, listening. That is enough. That is the X on the map. The rest is onion skins, violet petals, the small boat forever sailing across a windowsill of someone else’s making.

Word 2,500.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
